coredata.dc:title: “One stone, five birds”: Ultrabright and multifaceted carbon dots for precise cell imaging and glutathione detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zihao
Xu, Ke-Fei
Wang, Gang
Durrani, Samran
Lin, Fengming
Wu, Fu-Gen
coredata.dc:description: Developing a single system capable of realizing bacterial Gram-type identification, fungal cell imaging, endoplasmic reticulum (ER) imaging, glutathione (GSH) detection, and normal/cancer cell discrimination with excellent performance is a huge challenge. Herein, ultrabright green-emitting endoplasmic reticulum-targeting sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) with a quantum yield of ∼ 78 % were synthesized via the hydrothermal treatment of rose bengal (RB) and dl-cysteine (Cys). The obtained SCDs possessed excellent fluorescence stability, unique excitation-independent emission property, and satisfactory biocompatibility. We demonstrated that the SCDs could be used for fast (15 min) identification of Gram-positive bacteria due to their S-containing groups (e.g., sulfate and sulfydryl groups) and high-quality fungal cell imaging. We also demonstrated that the SCDs could realize selective and long-term ER imaging in normal/cancer cells, and achieve the monitoring of the cell behaviors during ER stress caused by starvation or various ER stress inducers in live cells. Further, by mixing SCDs and Cu2+, we prepared Cu/SCD nanocomposites (Cu/SCDs), in which the fluorescence of SCDs was quenched by Cu2+ and could recover upon the addition of GSH, realizing GSH detection with a limit of detection of 0.681 μM. Subsequently, based on the much higher GSH level of cancer cells than that of normal cells, Cu/SCDs were successfully applied for distinguishing normal cells from cancer ones. Overall, this work fabricates ultrabright and multifaceted SCDs for precise and high-quality cell imaging and GSH detection, confirming the great potential of SCDs for versatile biomedical applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustrating the fabrication process of SCDs and Cu/SCDs and their applications for (1) bacterial Gram-type identification, (2) fungal cell imaging, (3) ER imaging, (4) GSH detection, and (5) normal/cancer cell discrimination.

Characterization and application of SCDs. (a and b) TEM images of SCDs at different resolutions (the area shown in b was the same one marked by an orange square line of dashes in a) and (c) corresponding size distribution histogram. (d) AFM image of SCDs and (e) corresponding thickness of the SCDs marked by the dotted line. (f) UV–vis absorption spectrum of SCDs dispersed in PBS. (g) (αhν)1/2 versus hν curve of SCDs. The dashed line is tangential to the curve and the intersection value is E g. (h) Excitation–emission contour plot of the SCD dispersions (solvent: PBS). (i) Fluorescence stability evaluation results of the SCD dispersion (solvent: PBS, concentration: 100 μg/mL) under storage at room temperature for different time periods. Inset: Photographs of a representative SCD dispersion (solvent: PBS, concentration: 100 μg/mL) under UV light illumination (302 nm) at day 0 and day 14. (j) Scheme showing the anti-counterfeiting application of SCDs by using SCDs as the fluorescent ink via inkjet printing and the resultant printed pattern under the illumination of daylight or 302 nm UV light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Chemical composition characterization of SCDs. (a) Full-range XPS curve. (b–g) High-resolution XPS curves of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, S 2p, I 3d, and Cl 2p, respectively. (h) FTIR spectra of RB, SCDs, and Cys.

Fast identification of the Gram types of bacteria. (a and b) Confocal microscopic images of four Gram-positive bacteria including S. aureus, B. subtilis, M. luteus, and S. epidermidis and four Gram-negative bacteria including E. coli, P. aeruginosa, P. vulgaris, and S. marcescens. Before imaging, these bacteria were incubated by SCDs (50 μg/mL) for 15 min. (c and d) Flow cytometric results of the different bacteria incubated with SCDs with different concentrations for different time periods.

(a) Molecular structures of Hcy, Asp, Phe, Ser, and Ala. Confocal microscopic images (b and c) and flow cytometric results (d–f) of the representative Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) and the representative Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) incubated with HcyCDs, AspCDs, PheCDs, SerCDs, and AlaCDs, respectively, for 15 min.

(a) Relative viabilities of the 4T1 cells and HUVECs treated with SCDs at different concentrations for 24 h. (b) Hemolysis rates of red blood cells treated with SCDs at different concentrations. Triton X-100 treatment group was set as a positive control. (c, e, and f) Flow cytometric results and (d) confocal microscopic images of 4T1 cells and HUVECs incubated with SCDs (100 μg/mL) for different time periods. (g) Confocal microscopic images of SCDs (100 μg/mL)-incubated A549 cells costained with Lyso-Red (0.5 μM; for lysosome imaging), Mito-Deep Red (1 μM; for mitochondrion imaging), or ER-Red (1 μM; for ER imaging). Intensity correlation plots of SCDs and commercial dyes were shown in the last column. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal microscopic images of (a) normal cells (L02, HPAEpiC, NIH/3T3, L929, and HUVEC) and (b) cancer cells (A549, HeLa, 4T1, Hep G2, and MC38). Before imaging, the cells were incubated with SCDs (100 μg/mL) for 20 min and ER-Red (1 μM) for 15 min. Intensity correlation plots of SCDs and ER-Red were shown in the last column. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Confocal microscopic images of 4T1 cells continuously irradiated under a 552 nm laser for different time periods. Before imaging, the 4T1 cells were coincubated with SCDs (100 μg/mL) for 20 min and ER-Red (1 μM) for 15 min. (b) Confocal microscopic images of the A549 cells stained with SCDs (100 μg/mL) only (control) or treated with SCDs (100 μg/mL) and 4 ℃/one of the endocytosis inhibitors (i.e., amiloride, MβCD, Gen, and CPZ), and (b) corresponding normalized fluorescence intensities obtained by flow cytometry. Before imaging, the A549 cells in 4 °C or inhibitor groups were treated with 4 °C or one of the endocytosis inhibitors (i.e., amiloride, MβCD, Gen, and CPZ) for 2 h, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Confocal microscopic images of A549 cells stained with SCDs (100 μg/mL). Before imaging, the A549 cells in the starvation/drug-treated groups were treated with serum-free culture medium or serum-containing culture medium containing one of the ER stress inducers (i.e., Tm, Tg, DEX, DTT, and b-AP15) for 2/4 h, and (b–d) corresponding flow cytometric results.

Construction of Cu/SCDs for GSH detection and normal/cancer cell discrimination. (a) Scheme illustrating the construction of Cu/SCDs for GSH detection. rSCDs: recovered SCDs. (b) Fluorescence (FL) intensity of SCDs (100 μg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra and (d) intensities of Cu/SCDs (SCD concentration: 100 μg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of GSH. (e) Linear relationship between F/F 0 versus GSH concentration. (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu/SCDs (SCD concentration: 25 μg/mL), Cu/SCDs (SCD concentration: 25 μg/mL) + GSH (0.4 mM), and Cu2+ (50 mM) + GSH (0.4 mM). Solvent: water. (g) Zeta potentials of SCDs (25 μg/mL), Cu/SCDs (SCD concentration: 25 μg/mL), and Cu/SCDs (SCD concentration: 25 μg/mL) + GSH (0.4 mM). Solvent: water. (h) Left panel: TEM image of Cu/SCDs. Right panel: An enlarged TEM image of the Cu/SCD marked by a white dashed circle in the left panel. (i) Size distribution histogram of Cu/SCDs. (j) Left panel: TEM image of Cu/SCDs + GSH. Right panel: An enlarged TEM image of the Cu/SCDs + GSH marked by a white dashed circle in the left panel. (k) Confocal microscopic images of three normal cell lines (HPAEpiCs, NIH/3T3 cells, and L02 cells) and three cancer cell lines (A549, 4T1, and HeLa cells) incubated with SCDs (50 μg/mL) or Cu/SCDs (SCD concentration: 50 μg/mL) for 15 min and (l) corresponding flow cytometric results.
Atomic numbers of an RB molecule, a Cys molecule, and RB + Cys, as well as the atomic percentages of SCDs.

head.ce:title: “One stone, five birds”: Ultrabright and multifaceted carbon dots for precise cell imaging and glutathione detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a single system capable of realizing bacterial Gram-type identification, fungal cell imaging, endoplasmic reticulum (ER) imaging, glutathione (GSH) detection, and normal/cancer cell discrimination with excellent performance is a huge challenge. Herein, ultrabright green-emitting endoplasmic reticulum-targeting sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) with a quantum yield of ∼ 78 % were synthesized via the hydrothermal treatment of rose bengal (RB) and dl-cysteine (Cys). The obtained SCDs possessed excellent fluorescence stability, unique excitation-independent emission property, and satisfactory biocompatibility. We demonstrated that the SCDs could be used for fast (15 min) identification of Gram-positive bacteria due to their S-containing groups (e.g., sulfate and sulfydryl groups) and high-quality fungal cell imaging. We also demonstrated that the SCDs could realize selective and long-term ER imaging in normal/cancer cells, and achieve the monitoring of the cell behaviors during ER stress caused by starvation or various ER stress inducers in live cells. Further, by mixing SCDs and Cu2+, we prepared Cu/SCD nanocomposites (Cu/SCDs), in which the fluorescence of SCDs was quenched by Cu2+ and could recover upon the addition of GSH, realizing GSH detection with a limit of detection of 0.681 μM. Subsequently, based on the much higher GSH level of cancer cells than that of normal cells, Cu/SCDs were successfully applied for distinguishing normal cells from cancer ones. Overall, this work fabricates ultrabright and multifaceted SCDs for precise and high-quality cell imaging and GSH detection, confirming the great potential of SCDs for versatile biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic infections, particularly those caused by Gram-positive bacteria, severely threaten human health, and therefore sensitive, rapid, and accurate identification of the Gram types of live bacteria is of tremendous significance for the fundamental research in microbiology and the treatment of microbial infections [1]. To date, some traditional methods (e.g., immunological techniques [2], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [3], and nucleic acid detection [4]) have been developed for the identification of bacterial Gram types. Disappointingly, most of these approaches are expensive, complex, and time-consuming. Fluorescence detection approaches possess many merits including rapid response, high sensitivity, and the capability to be simultaneously used with other fluorescent probes emitting fluorescence with different wavelengths for multiple analyses [5].

As an emerging type of quasi-0D carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted ever-increasing attention since their discovery in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [6]. In the past decade, CDs have been extensively studied and applied in numerous fields, such as light-emitting diodes [7–10], sensing [11–13], catalysis [14–16], bioimaging [17–22], antibacterial application [23–28], drug delivery [29,30], fingerprint identification [31–33], and information encryption [34–36], as a result of their distinctive advantages (e.g., high photostability, tunable luminescence emission, satisfactory biocompatibility, and easy surface modification property) [37–41]. In previous studies, we have prepared aggregation-induced emission fluorogens (AIEgens), quaternized silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs), and quaternized CDs for the identification of Gram-positive bacteria [23,24,42,43]. However, synthesizing the AIEgens demands complex organic synthesis processes and expensive raw materials, and the quaternized SiNPs and quaternized CDs possess high antibacterial activities, which can destroy the complete structures of live bacterial cells and are not conducive to precise microbiology research. Recently, Yan et al. prepared peptidoglycan-targeting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by hydrothermal treatment of glucose, glycine, and l-tryptophan for the discrimination and continuous tracking of live Gram-positive bacteria [44]. However, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the peptidoglycan-targeting CQDs was not very high (∼ 23 %), which may somewhat affect the identification accuracy of fluorescence analysis. Therefore, developing fluorescent probes with high QYs and the ability to sensitively, rapidly, and accurately identify the Gram types of live bacteria is urgently needed.

On the other hand, endoplasmic reticulum (ER), the largest organelle in eukaryotic cells, serves as an intracellular endomembrane system governing many vital cellular processes, such as the synthesis, processing, and transportation of membrane-associated and secreted proteins, the biosynthesis and transport of lipids, as well as the maintenance and regulation of calcium homeostasis [45–48]. The accumulation of unfolded or misfolded proteins in ER induced by some stimuli (e.g., starvation, oxidative stress, hypoxia, and pharmacological stress inducers) can cause the ER stress, activating the unfolded protein response (UPR) [49,50]. UPR is an adaptive signaling pathway for improving the protein folding ability of cells, and is related to many serious diseases including cancer, Alzheimer’s disease, liver disease, Parkinson’s disease, type 2 diabetes, etc. [47,51,52]. Therefore, selective and precise ER imaging is crucial for the monitoring of ER stress and the investigation of ER-related physiological processes. Nevertheless, the frequently-used commercial ER-Tracker probes (e.g., ER-Tracker Green/Red) contain the glibenclamide moiety with unique pharmacological activity, which may exert unavoidable side effects on the normal functions and activities of ER and influence other cellular activities [49]. In addition, other common ER-targeting fluorescent probes (e.g., 3,3′-dihexyloxacarbocyanine iodide) and metal-containing ER-targeting probes exhibit potential cytotoxicity/phototoxicity, and are thus not suitable for long-term ER imaging [53]. Besides, the existing ER-targeting CDs generally face the following shortcomings: the requirement of introduction of ER-targeting moieties [54], low QYs [55], complex purification processes [56,57], and low selectivity [58]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to synthesize ER-targeting CDs with high QYs, satisfactory biocompatibility, stable fluorescence, and outstanding ER affinity and selectivity without the introduction of ER-targeting moieties via simple preparation methods for ER imaging and stress monitoring.

As for the intracellular overexpressed glutathione (GSH), a biochemical characteristic of many cancer cells [59], its efficient and accurate detection is significant for the discrimination between cancer and normal cells, and its depletion can destroy intracellular redox homeostasis, which may elicit potential cytotoxicity to cancer cells. In recent years, various GSH detection and depletion strategies have been developed for normal/cancer cell discrimination [60–62] and cancer therapy [63–66]. Nevertheless, many GSH detection strategies require complicated operation steps and possess unsatisfactory detection sensitivity, and the probes for normal/cancer cell discrimination usually have poor discrimination effect, limiting their further biomedical applications. Therefore, a robust probe with the capacity to detect GSH with a low limit of detection (LOD) and effectively discriminate cancer cells from normal cells is highly needed.

Here, we fabricated green-emitting sulfur-doped CDs (termed SCDs) with a high QY of ∼ 78 % via the hydrothermal treatment of rose bengal (RB) and dl-cysteine (Cys) (Fig. 1 ). The obtained SCDs possessed excellent fluorescence stability, unique excitation-independent emission property, and satisfactory biocompatibility, which were suitable for real-time and long-term bioimaging of live cells with high imaging quality and accuracy. After the hydrothermal reaction, the sulfhydryl groups of Cys were partly converted into sulfate groups, and these S-containing groups endowed the SCDs with the ability to rapidly (15 min) distinguish the Gram types of live bacteria. Furthermore, the SCDs could selectively target the ER of live normal cells (L02, HPAEpiC, NIH/3T3, L929, and HUVEC cells) and cancer cells (A549, HeLa, 4T1, Hep G2, and MC38 cells) without the need of additional modification of ER-targeting molecules. Moreover, we demonstrated that the endocytosis of SCDs by A549 cells (serving as a representative) was dominated by clathrin- and lipid raft-mediated endocytosis. Besides, the cellular behaviors during ER stress caused by starvation or various ER stress inducers in live cells were effectively monitored based on the fluorescence intensity, fluorescence distribution, and morphology of SCDs-stained cells. Furthermore, we prepared Cu2+/SCD nanocomposites (Cu/SCDs) via the self-assembly between SCDs and Cu2+ for GSH detection and normal/cancer cell discrimination since GSH in cancer cells could trigger the disassembly of Cu/SCDs and cause the “turn-on” of fluorescence. Consequently, the prepared SCDs and Cu/SCDs could be utilized for bacterial Gram type identification, fungal cell imaging, ER imaging, GSH detection, and normal/cancer cell discrimination, achieving the effect of “one stone, five birds”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SCDs and other amino acid-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mg RB and 0.6 mg Cys were added into 6 mL deionized (DI) water, and the mixed solution was transferred into a 10 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, the autoclave was heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h and cooled to room temperature. Afterwards, the obtained dispersion was purified via dialysis (molecular weight cut-off of the dialysis membrane: ∼ 1000 Da) against deionized (DI) water for 2 days and centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The precipitate was washed twice using DI water and dried under vacuum for 12 h. The obtained dried SCDs were dispersed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) or ethanol for further use and characterization. Other amino acid-derived CDs were fabricated similarly, but the Cys was replaced by dl-homocysteine (Hcy), dl-aspartic acid (Asp), dl-phenylalanine (Phe), dl-serine (Ser), and dl-alanine (Ala), respectively. In addition, other SCDs with different reaction conditions were prepared similarly with different reaction temperatures, reaction time periods, and RB/Cys molar ratios. To be noted, stirring the mixed solution containing RB and Cys in an open vessel at room temperature for at least 90 min before the hydrothermal treatment is capable of improving the ER targeting ability of the finally abtained SCDs, probably attributed to the oxidation of the sulfydryl group of Cys in air.

10 mL of SCD aqueous dispersion (500 μg/mL) was slowly added into 10 mL of CuCl2 solution (1 M), followed by continuous magnetic stirring at room temperature for 30 min, affording a dark blue dispersion. The concentration of SCDs in the obtained Cu/SCDs was determined to be ∼ 250 μg/mL.

The fluorescent pattern was printed by using a commercially available printer (HP DeskJet 1112) driven by a computer (ink: 200 μg/mL SCDs dispersed in PBS, paper: nonfluorescent paper). The printed fluorescent pattern was observed under 302 nm light illumination.

As shown in Fig. 1, the SCDs were fabricated through the hydrothermal treatment of the aqueous solution containing RB and Cys at 180 ℃ for 12 h. The resultant SCDs possessed a uniform size distribution with an average diameter of around 3.7 nm as revealed by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2 a) and the corresponding size distribution histogram (Fig. 2c). Besides, as shown in Fig. 2b, the high-resolution TEM image of an SCD marked by a dashed square in Fig. 2a revealed a lattice parameter of ∼ 0.24 nm. In addition, Fig. 2d showed a representative atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of SCDs, revealing that the thickness of the SCDs was approximately 1.5–3.5 nm (Fig. 2e). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of SCDs displayed wide absorption bands at UV and visible regions and a strong absorption peak centered at 511 nm (which may be assigned to the n–π* transition of trapping surface states related to CO) (Fig. 2f). Additionally, the optical band gap (E g) of SCDs was calculated to be 2.306 eV according to the Tauc plot using the relation between hν and (αhν) r (where h, ν, and α are the Planck constant, frequency of radiation, and absorption coefficient, respectively, and r = 1/2) (Fig. 2g). The two-dimensional excitation–emission fluorescence map (Fig. 2h) showed a remarkable single emission center with the optimal excitation/emission wavelength of 511/532 nm, and the fluorescence spectra of the SCDs under different excitation wavelengths (Figure S1) suggested that the fluorescence emission of the SCDs was excitation-independent. The QY of the SCDs was measured to be ∼ 78 % by using quinine sulfate (QY: 54 % in 0.1 % H2SO4) as a reference, and the QYs of the CDs prepared with different precursor ratios, reaction temperatures, and reaction time periods were shown in Table S1 and S2. The fluorescence stability evaluation results of the SCDs at different storage time points were shown in Fig. 2i and the inset of Fig. 2i displayed the photographs of the SCD dispersions (solvent: PBS) under UV light illumination (302 nm) at day 0 and day 14. The zeta potential of the SCDs was measured to be approximately –28 mV, suggesting that the SCDs were negatively charged. Benefiting from their high QY and stable fluorescence, SCDs were successfully used for anti-counterfeiting application. As shown in Fig. 2j, a fluorescent pattern of a QR (quick response) code was printed via inkjet printing by using SCD aqueous dispersion as the ink, and the fluorescent pattern only appeared under the UV irradiation but not under daylight illumination, suggesting that the SCDs with excellent photoluminescence properties can be used for anti-counterfeiting application via inkjet printing.

Furthermore, the chemical composition and functional groups of the SCDs were determined via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The full-range XPS curve (Fig. 3 a) of the SCDs revealed that C, N, O, S, Cl, I, and Na elements were present with the atomic percentages of 69.2 %, 1.4 %, 18.0 %, 0.5 %, 10.5 %, 0.3 %, and 0.1 %, respectively (Table 1 ). Besides, the atomic number of an RB molecule, a Cys molecule, and an RB molecule plus a Cys molecule (RB + Cys) were also shown in Table 1. The C/O percentage of SCDs (3.8) was higher than that of RB + Cys (2.3), indicating that RB and Cys molecules underwent carbonization and dehydration processes (due to the fact that the O element of RB and Cys molecules was lost in the form of water) during the hydrothermal treatment. Moreover, the high-resolution XPS curve of C 1 s displayed the peaks of CC/CC (284.5 eV), CN/CS (285.9 eV), CO (286.5 eV), CCl (288.7 eV), and CO/CN (291.8 eV) (Fig. 3b). The high-resolution XPS curve of N 1 s could be deconvoluted into two main peaks at 399.6 eV and 402.4 eV, which could be assigned to NH and CNC, respectively (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, three oxygen-containing functional groups of the SCDs including CO, CO/SO/SO, and OCO were detected in the high-resolution XPS curve of O 1 s. The S 2p spectrum of the SCDs displayed in Fig. 3e revealed the presence of SH and sulfates (CSO x
, x = 2, 3, and 4) at 163.8 and 168.1 eV, respectively. In addition, the XPS curves of I 3d and Cl 2p were shown in Fig. 3f and g, respectively. Furthermore, the FTIR spectra of RB, SCDs, and Cys were shown in Fig. 3h, and the results revealed the existence of NH, CO, CC, and CS structures in SCDs, in accordance with the XPS results. To be noted, the peak centered at ∼ 2630 cm−1 originating from the stretching vibration of SH could be detected in the FTIR spectrum of Cys instead of that of SCDs, suggesting that the sulfhydryl groups of Cys were oxidized into sulfates in the SCDs after the hydrothermal reaction.

To evaluate the Gram-type identification capability of the SCDs, four kinds of Gram-positive bacteria (i.e., Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), Micrococcus luteus (M. luteus), and Staphylococcus epidermidis (S. epidermidis)) and four types of Gram-negative bacteria (i.e., Escherichia coli (E. coli), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris), and Serratia marcescens (S. marcescens)) were chosen to be incubated with the SCDs for 15 min. The confocal microscopic images of the four Gram-positive bacteria (Fig. 4 a) and the four Gram-negative bacteria (Fig. 4b) indicated that all the four Gram-positive bacteria were stained by the SCDs and emitted strong green fluorescence under the excitation of a 488 nm laser, while no fluorescence signal was detected from the four Gram-negative bacteria. As revealed by the flow cytometric results of the Gram-positive bacteria (Fig. 4c), the fluorescence intensities of the stained Gram-positive bacteria were concentration- and time-dependent, and therefore the SCD concentration of 50 μg/mL and incubation time of 15 min were chosen as the further experimental conditions for bacterial imaging. Moreover, the flow cytometric results of the different Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Fig. 4c and d) also indicated that the SCDs were capable of selectively staining Gram-positive bacteria and could be used as a fluorescent probe for fast Gram-type identification. Furthermore, Figure S2 revealed that the SCDs exerted no inhibition effect on the activities of S. aureus and E. coli, indicating that the SCDs possessed no bacterial toxicity and were suitable for fluorescence imaging of live bacteria.

To investigate the determinant factors of the SCDs’ Gram-type identification capability, the raw material Cys was replaced by Hcy, Asp, Phe, Ser, and Ala, respectively, and their molecular structures were shown in Fig. 5 a (the obtained CDs were termed HcyCDs, AspCDs, PheCDs, SerCDs, and AlaCDs, respectively). Then, these CDs (50 μg/mL) were also used to stain the representative Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) and Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) for 15 min, respectively. The confocal microscopic imaging results (Fig. 5b and c) revealed that only HcyCDs derived from the sulfhydryl group-containing Hcy could selectively stain live S. aureus cells, while the others possessed no Gram-type identification capability, which was also verified by the corresponding flow cytometric results (Fig. 5d–f). Furthermore, the zeta potentials of HcyCDs, AspCDs, PheCDs, SerCDs, and AlaCDs were measured to be around –32.6, –57.0, –45.0, –41.4, and –22.9 mV (Figure S3), revealing that these CDs were all negatively charged, which was similar to SCDs. These results suggested that, on the SCD surface, the sulfate structure originating from the sulfhydryl group of Cys may determine the Gram-type identification capability of the finally synthesized CDs.

Besides the above bacterial cells, live fungal cells were also incubated with SCDs (50 μg/mL) for 15 min. As displayed in Figure S4, three types of fungal cells including yeast, Candida albicans (C. albicans), and Trichoderma reesei (T. reesei) all emitted strong green fluorescence under 488 nm laser excitation, suggesting that the prepared CDs could serve as a fluorescent probe for the fluorescence imaging of fungal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity evaluation, time-dependent flow cytometric analysis and confocal imaging, and organelle localization analysis of live cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before the cell imaging experiments, the cytotoxicity of SCDs was evaluated via 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay and hemolysis experiment. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the MTT results revealed that the relative viabilities of cancer cells (4T1 cells) and normal cells (human umbilical vein endothelial cells, HUVECs) were over 90 % at a concentration of 100 μg/mL or below. Besides, the hemolysis assay results (Fig. 6b) suggested that the SCDs showed negligible hemolytic effect when the concentration of the SCDs was up to 100 μg/mL. These results demonstrated that the SCDs possessed excellent biocompatibility and were suitable for animal cell imaging. The time-dependent flow cytometric analysis (Fig. 6c, e, and f) and confocal imaging (Fig. 6d) of cancer cells (4T1 cells) and normal cells (HUVECs) indicated that SCDs were rapidly internalized by cells and reached an appropriate internalization amount for fluorescence imaging at 10 min, and afterwards the internalized SCDs were excreted or digested. Subsequently, colocalization experiments were carried out by separately staining A549 cells with SCDs and each of the following commercial organelle-targeting dyes: LysoTracker Red (Lyso-Red, targeting lysosome), MitoTracker Deep Red (Mito-Deep Red, targeting mitochondrion), and ER-Tracker Red (ER-Red, targeting ER). As shown in Fig. 6g, the merged confocal microscopic images clearly revealed the excellent colocalization of SCDs and ER-Red. In addition, the PCC (Pearson’s correlation coefficient) and OLC (overlap coefficient) values of the SCDs–ER-Red group (0.93 and 0.94, respectively) were much higher than those of the SCDs–Lyso-Red group (0.21 and 0.25, respectively) and the SCDs–Mito-Deep Red group (0.59 and 0.62, respectively), suggesting the outstanding ER affinity and targeting selectivity of SCDs.

To demonstrate the universal ER imaging capability of SCDs, five kinds of normal cells (i.e., L02, HPAEpiC, NIH/3T3, L929, and HUVEC) and five types of cancer cells (i.e., A549, HeLa, 4T1, Hep G2, and MC38) were chosen to be stained with SCDs for 20 min and ER-Red for 15 min, successively. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the merged confocal images clearly indicated the good colocalization of SCDs and ER-Red in different cell lines. Besides, the PCC and OLC values of these cell lines were all larger than 0.93, suggesting that the SCDs possessed universal and excellent ER targeting capacity toward live cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability evaluation and endocytosis mechanism assay
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability of SCDs in cells was evaluated and the results were shown in Fig. 8 a. The 4T1 cells pretreated with SCDs and ER-Red were continuously irradiated using a 552 nm laser (10 % of the maximum output of the laser of the confocal microscope) for 20 min. It could be observed that the red fluorescence signals from ER-Red were rapidly weakened and disappeared in 10 min, while the green fluorescence signals from the SCDs accumulating at ER could still be notably seen after laser irradiation for 20 min, indicating the high photostability of the SCDs, which was beneficial for long-term ER imaging.

In general, the endocytosis pathways can be divided into four types (i.e., macropinocytosis, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis (LrME), clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CME), and caveolae-mediated endocytosis (CvME)). Therefore, 5-(N,N-dimethyl)-amiloride hydrochloride (amiloride, an inhibitor of macropinocytosis), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD, an inhibitor of LrME), genistein (Gen, an inhibitor of CvME), and chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPZ, an inhibitor of CME) were used to investigate the endocytosis pathways of SCDs in A549 cells. Besides, 4 ℃ treatment was also adopted to verify if the cellular uptake of SCDs was energy-dependent. The confocal microscopic images and flow cytometric assay results shown in Fig. 8b and c revealed that, compared with the control group, the fluorescence intensities of the amiloride, MβCD, 4 ℃, Gen, and CPZ groups decreased by around 7.1 %, 28.9 %, 82.0 %, 1.1 %, and 63.6 %, respectively. Therefore, the cellular uptake of SCDs was confirmed to be energy-dependent and the endocytosis pathways could be mainly ascribed to CME and LrME.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of cells during ER stress
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the excellent ER imaging ability of SCDs, we further applied SCDs to monitor the cellular behaviors during the ER stress caused by starvation or one of the following ER stress inducers: tunicamycin (Tm, an inhibitor of glycosylation during the synthesis of glycolipids and proteins), thapsigargin (Tg, an inhibitor of sarco/endoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase), dexamethasone (DEX, a cell apoptosis inducer), dithiothreitol (DTT, a strong reducing agent that can interfere with the synthesis of protein disulfide bonds), and 2-N,4-N-dibenzylquinazoline-2,4-diamine (b-AP15, a deubiquitinase inhibitor). As shown in Fig. 9 a, compared with the cells in the control group, the cells treated with Tm for 2/4 h exhibited decreased fluorescence intensity and similar fluorescence distribution (mainly ER). By contrast, the cells treated with Tg for 2/4 h both possessed obvious green fluorescence signals in the nuclear regions, suggesting that their nuclear structures may be damaged during the Tg-induced ER stress. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of the cells in the Tg group was the lowest compared with that in other groups, as revealed by the flow cytometric results (Fig. 9b–d). In addition, DEX could result in the formation of black regions without fluorescence signals, indicating that the ER structure may be partly destroyed. To be noted, the cells in the DTT group were round in shape, indicating that DTT could change the normal cell morphology. Moreover, b-AP15 could cause the emergence of green fluorescent nucleus with bright green points, which might be SCDs stained-nucleoli. Furthermore, the starvation-induced ER stress only resulted in the apparent decrease of fluorescence intensity, which could be attributed to the physiological change (e.g., polarity and pH) of ER, and no obvious morphological change of cells was observed after starvation treatment. Consequently, the cellular behaviors during various types of ER stress were effectively monitored by using SCDs as an ER imaging probe, and different types of ER stress induced by different stimuli could be effectively discriminated based on the cells’ fluorescence intensity, fluorescence distribution, and morphology through confocal microscopic imaging and flow cytometric analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of Cu/SCDs for GSH detection and normal/cancer cell discrimination
ce:section.ce:para: Cu/SCDs were constructed via the self-assembly (mainly via the electrostatic interaction and coordination interaction) of SCDs and Cu2+ (Fig. 10 a), in which the fluorescence intensity of SCDs was decreased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 10b), and the fluorescence intensity was increased upon the subsequent addition of GSH (Fig. 10c and d). This GSH-induced fluorescence enhancement could be ascribed to the disassembly of Cu/SCDs followed by the release of ultrabright recovered SCDs (rSCDs) from Cu/SCDs, and the chemical structures and FL emission centers of SCDs might be changed, resulting in the change of rSCDs’ emission peak shape and intensity. Then, the GSH detection selectivity of Cu/SCDs was analyzed, and the results displayed in Figure S5 indicated that Cu/SCDs exhibited satisfactory detection selectivity toward GSH without the notable interference from various biomolecules and ions. Based on the above results, Cu/SCDs were successfully applied for GSH detection with a linear detection range from 0 to 100 μM and an LOD of 0.681 μM (Fig. 10e). Then, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu/SCDs, Cu/SCDs + GSH, and Cu2+ + GSH were measured and shown in Fig. 10f. The absorption spectrum of Cu/SCDs possessed a small absorption peak centered at ∼ 500 nm (originating from SCDs) and a characteristic absorption band of copper ions from 600 to 900 nm (the absorption spectrum of copper ions was displayed in Figure S6). After the addition of GSH, whose absorption spectrum was shown in Figure S7, the absorption spectrum of Cu/SCDs + GSH displayed a greatly enhanced absorbance intensity, which was similar to that of Cu2+ + GSH, revealing that the reaction between GSH and Cu2+ dominated the reaction between GSH and Cu/SCDs. Furthermore, the zeta potential values of SCDs, Cu/SCDs, and Cu/SCDs + GSH were measured to be –16.6, –4.5, and 0.9 mV, respectively (Fig. 10g), suggesting that Cu2+ and GSH could successfully interact with SCDs and Cu/SCDs, respectively. Then, the morphologies of Cu/SCDs and Cu/SCDs + GSH were observed via TEM. The TEM images of Cu/SCDs revealed that the Cu/SCDs possessed an average size of 24.5 ± 9.6 nm, and the enlarged image of a Cu/SCD displayed that the Cu/SCD was composed of aggregated SCDs (Fig. 10h and i). After the addition of GSH, Cu/SCDs were disintegrated and the aggregated SCDs were released from Cu/SCDs (Fig. 10j). Subsequently, SCDs and Cu/SCDs were separately incubated with three normal cell lines (HPAEpiCs, NIH/3T3 cells, and L02 cells) and three cancer cell lines (A549 cells, 4T1 cells, and HeLa cells). The confocal microscopic images (Fig. 10k) indicated that, in the Cu/SCDs group, all the normal cells possessed negligible fluorescence while all the cancer cells emitted strong green fluorescence, which could be ascribed to the disassembly of Cu/SCDs induced by the intracellular overexpressed GSH of cancer cells. By contrast, SCDs possessed no normal/cancer cell discrimination ability. Additionally, these results were consistent with the flow cytometric results (Fig. 10l), indicating that Cu/SCDs were capable of effectively discriminating between cancer and normal cells.

In this work, we fabricated a type of ER-targeting SCDs with a high QY of ∼ 78 % via the hydrothermal treatment of RB and Cys. The obtained SCDs possessed the advantages of high fluorescence stability, unique excitation-independent fluorescence emission, and satisfactory biocompatibility. Furthermore, we verified that the surface sulfates endowed the SCDs with the ability to rapidly and sensitively distinguish live Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus, B. subtilis, M. luteus, and S. epidermidis) from live Gram-negative ones (E. coli, P. aeruginosa, P. vulgaris, and S. marcescens). Besides, the SCDs could selectively target and accumulate at ER when they were internalized in live normal cells (L02, HPAEpiC, NIH/3T3, L929, and HUVEC) or cancerous cells (A549, HeLa, 4T1, Hep G2, and MC38), and realize stable, long-term, and high-quality ER imaging. Moreover, using A549 cells as an example, we revealed that the endocytosis pathway of SCDs was dominated by CME and LrME. In addition, by using SCDs as an ER imaging probe, the cellular behaviors during ER stress were effectively monitored, and different types of ER stress induced by drugs or starvation could be discriminated through fluorescence imaging and flow cytometric analysis. Furthermore, by mixing SCDs and Cu2+, we constructed Cu/SCDs and demonstrated that they could realize GSH-induced structural disassembly and fluorescence recovery, which was adopted for GSH detection and normal/cancer cell discrimination. This work represents an interesting example that a single type of CDs can realize versatile applications including bacterial Gram-type identification, fungal cell imaging, ER imaging, GSH detection, and normal/cancer cell discrimination, which we believe may promote the further development of multifunctional CDs for desirable uses.
